courses.title: API Fundamentals
courses.description: Master the Claude Messages API: authentication, model selection, streaming responses, and core parameters that control output quality.

lessons.title: Authentication & Your First API Call
lessons.description: Set up the Anthropic SDK, authenticate with your API key, and send your first message to Claude.

sections.content:
Authentication & Your First API Call

The Anthropic SDK is available for TypeScript/JavaScript and Python. We focus on TypeScript throughout this track.

Installation

npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk

Authentication

The SDK reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from your environment automatically:

import Anthropic from '@anthropic-ai/sdk';
const client = new Anthropic();

Your First Message

const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Hello, Claude!' }]
});
console.log(response.content[0].text);

The response contains content (array of content blocks), stop_reason, and usage (input/output token counts).

exercises.question: Write a function that sends a message to Claude and returns the text response.
exercises.explanation: The Messages API requires model, max_tokens, and messages. Always check content[0].type before accessing .text.

exercises.question: What does a stop_reason of 'max_tokens' indicate?
options: The request exceeded the rate limit
Claude's response was truncated at the max_tokens limit
The input prompt was too long
exercises.explanation: stop_reason 'max_tokens' means Claude was still generating but hit the ceiling. Increase max_tokens or make a follow-up request.

exercises.question: Write a function that returns total token usage (input + output) from a response.
exercises.explanation: Every response includes usage.input_tokens and usage.output_tokens. Track these for cost management.

lessons.title: Model Selection & Parameters
lessons.description: Choose the right Claude model and tune temperature, top_p, and top_k.

sections.content:
Model Selection & Parameters

Choosing the right model is one of the most impactful decisions you will make when building with the Claude API. Each model in the family is optimized for a different balance of capability, speed, and cost, and picking the wrong one leads to either overspending or underperforming.

  • claude-opus-4-20250514 — Most capable, complex reasoning. Use for tasks that require deep analysis, nuanced writing, or multi-step logic. Opus is the model you reach for when accuracy matters more than speed or cost.
  • claude-sonnet-4-20250514 — Balanced performance. Great default for most production use cases: chatbots, code generation, document summarization. Sonnet gives you strong quality at a reasonable price point.
  • claude-haiku-3-5-20241022 — Fastest and cheapest. Ideal for high-volume tasks like classification, entity extraction, and simple Q&A. When you are processing thousands of items, Haiku keeps your bill manageable.

Temperature

Temperature controls randomness in Claude's output. Range 0.0-1.0. Use 0.0 for deterministic tasks where you want the same output every time (classification, data extraction, code generation). Use 0.7-1.0 for creative tasks where variety is desirable (brainstorming, creative writing). A common pitfall is using high temperature for structured output — if you need reliable JSON, always set temperature to 0.

Other Parameters

top_p and top_k offer additional control over sampling but are rarely needed. In practice, temperature alone handles most use cases. The max_tokens parameter caps the response length — set it to match your expected output size to avoid paying for tokens you do not need. For instance, a classification task only needs max_tokens of 10-50, while a code generation task might need 2000-4000.

const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-haiku-3-5-20241022',
  max_tokens: 256,
  temperature: 0.0,
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Extract the email from: Contact hello@example.com' }]
});

exercises.question: Which model is best for high-volume classification where speed and cost matter most?
exercises.explanation: Haiku is fastest and cheapest, ideal for classification, extraction, and high-volume tasks.

exercises.question: Write a function that classifies feedback as 'positive', 'negative', or 'neutral' using Haiku with temperature 0.
exercises.explanation: Temperature 0 ensures deterministic output. Low max_tokens saves cost. Always validate the response matches expected values.

exercises.question: What temperature should you use for deterministic code generation?
exercises.explanation: Temperature 0.0 always selects the highest-probability token, producing consistent deterministic output.

lessons.title: System Prompts & Multi-turn Messages
lessons.description: Craft system prompts and manage multi-turn conversation history.

sections.content:
System Prompts & Multi-turn Messages

The system prompt is a top-level parameter, not part of the messages array:

await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  system: 'You are a TypeScript expert. Respond only with code.',
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Write a debounce function' }]
});

Multi-turn

Messages alternate user/assistant. You manage the full history:

messages: [
  { role: 'user', content: 'What is TypeScript?' },
  { role: 'assistant', content: 'TypeScript is...' },
  { role: 'user', content: 'How does it compare to Flow?' }
]

Prefilling

Prefill the assistant response to steer output format:

messages: [
  { role: 'user', content: 'List 3 benefits as JSON' },
  { role: 'assistant', content: '[' }
]

exercises.question: Write a chat function that appends a user message to history, calls the API, appends the response, and returns updated history.
exercises.explanation: The SDK is stateless. You pass full conversation history each call. This is the foundation of every chatbot.

exercises.question: Where does the system prompt go in a Messages API request?
exercises.explanation: Anthropic uses a top-level 'system' parameter, cleanly separating instructions from conversation history.

exercises.question: Use assistant prefilling to extract name and age as JSON from a text description.
exercises.explanation: Prefilling with '{' forces Claude to continue a JSON object. Prepend the prefix when parsing. This predates JSON mode but still works for simple cases.

lessons.title: Streaming Responses
lessons.description: Stream tokens in real-time using Server-Sent Events for responsive UIs.

sections.content:
Streaming Responses

Without streaming, your application waits in silence until Claude finishes generating its entire response — which can take several seconds for long outputs. Streaming delivers tokens as they are generated, giving users immediate feedback and making your application feel responsive. This is especially important for chat interfaces, where a blank screen creates an awkward pause.

The Anthropic SDK makes streaming straightforward with the stream() method, which returns an event emitter you can listen to:

const stream = client.messages.stream({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Write a haiku' }]
});
stream.on('text', (delta) => process.stdout.write(delta));
const final = await stream.finalMessage();

Event Types

Under the hood, streaming uses Server-Sent Events (SSE). The SDK abstracts most of this, but understanding the event types helps when debugging:

  • message_start — message metadata (model, usage estimate)
  • content_block_delta — incremental text (text_delta) or tool input (input_json_delta)
  • message_delta — stop_reason, final usage counts
  • message_stop — stream complete

When to Stream

Stream whenever a human is waiting for the response — chat UIs, code editors, content generators. Do not stream for background processing (batch jobs, automated pipelines) where you just need the final result. A common mistake is forgetting to call finalMessage() — this returns the complete Message object with accurate usage statistics, which you need for cost tracking.

exercises.question: Stream a response, collect full text, and return it with output token count.
exercises.explanation: stream.on('text') fires per delta. finalMessage() waits for completion and gives the full Message with usage stats.

exercises.question: Which event carries incremental text tokens?
exercises.explanation: Text arrives as content_block_delta events with delta.type 'text_delta'. The text is in delta.text.

exercises.question: Write a streaming function that calls an onToken callback for each delta, for piping to a UI.
exercises.explanation: The callback pattern separates delivery from collection. The callback can update a DOM element, push to a WebSocket, or set React state.

lessons.title: Vision & Multi-modal Input
lessons.description: Send images to Claude for analysis, OCR, and visual reasoning.

sections.content:
Vision & Multi-modal Input

Claude can analyze images alongside text, opening up use cases like document processing, chart interpretation, UI screenshot analysis, and OCR. Images are sent as content blocks within a message, either base64-encoded or via URL. This is the same Messages API you already know — you simply include image blocks alongside text blocks in the content array.

import fs from 'fs';
const imageData = fs.readFileSync('chart.png').toString('base64');

const r = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  messages: [{
    role: 'user',
    content: [
      { type: 'image', source: { type: 'base64', media_type: 'image/png', data: imageData } },
      { type: 'text', text: 'Describe this chart.' }
    ]
  }]
});

Image Sources

You can provide images in two ways: base64 for local files (read from disk, encode, and send inline) or URL for images hosted online. URL sources are simpler but require the image to be publicly accessible. Base64 works for any image but increases request size.

Supported Formats

Claude supports JPEG, PNG, GIF, and WebP. SVG and TIFF are not supported. Keep in mind that image tokens scale with resolution — a 4000x4000 image costs significantly more tokens than a 400x400 one. For cost-sensitive applications, resize images before sending them. You can include multiple images in a single message, which is useful for comparison tasks like 'which of these two designs is better?' or 'find the differences between these screenshots.'

Common Pitfalls

The most common mistake is getting the media_type wrong — if your file is a JPEG but you specify image/png, you will get an error. Another pitfall is sending very large images when a smaller version would suffice, unnecessarily increasing token costs.

exercises.question: Write an OCR function that reads an image file and returns extracted text.
exercises.explanation: Image blocks need media_type matching the format. Base64-encode the binary data. Token cost scales with resolution.

exercises.question: Which image formats does Claude support?
exercises.explanation: Claude supports JPEG, PNG, GIF, and WebP. SVG and TIFF are not supported.

exercises.question: Send two URL images to Claude for comparison in a single message.
exercises.explanation: Multiple images go as separate content blocks. Claude reasons across all images in a single pass.

courses.title: Tool Use & Function Calling
courses.description: Give Claude the ability to call your functions: define tools with inputSchema, handle tool_use responses, and control tool selection.

lessons.title: Defining Tools with inputSchema
lessons.description: Define tools as JSON Schema objects that Claude can invoke.

sections.content:
Defining Tools

Tool use is what transforms Claude from a text generator into an agent that can take actions in the real world. Tools are functions you define that Claude can choose to call — checking a database, sending an email, searching the web, or anything else your application needs. You describe each tool with a name, a description, and an input_schema (JSON Schema) that defines what parameters it accepts:

const tools = [{
  name: 'get_weather',
  description: 'Get current weather for a city',
  input_schema: {
    type: 'object',
    properties: {
      city: { type: 'string', description: 'City name' },
      units: { type: 'string', enum: ['celsius','fahrenheit'], description: 'Temperature units' }
    },
    required: ['city']
  }
}];

Why Descriptions Matter

The description is the single most important field. Claude uses it to decide when to call the tool, how to fill in parameters, and what to expect back. A vague description like 'Gets weather' leads to incorrect tool selection. A good description includes what the tool does, what it returns, and when to use it. For instance: 'Get current weather conditions for a city. Returns temperature, humidity, and conditions. Use when the user asks about weather or needs outdoor activity recommendations.'

Schema Design Tips

Use enum to constrain values (prevents Claude from inventing invalid options). Add description to every property so Claude understands what each parameter means. Mark truly optional parameters as optional — do not make everything required. A common mistake is omitting the required array, which makes all parameters optional and can lead to Claude skipping critical inputs.

exercises.question: Define a tool schema for a 'search_database' function that takes a query string and an optional limit number.
exercises.explanation: Tools use JSON Schema for input_schema. Required fields go in the required array. Descriptions help Claude use tools correctly.

exercises.question: What is the most important factor in helping Claude use a tool correctly?
exercises.explanation: The description is how Claude understands what the tool does, when to use it, and what it returns. Clear descriptions dramatically improve tool selection accuracy.

exercises.question: Define two tools: 'create_task' (title, priority enum, optional due_date) and 'list_tasks' (optional status filter).
exercises.explanation: Multiple tools let Claude pick the right one for each situation. Enum constraints prevent invalid values.

lessons.title: The Tool Use Loop
lessons.description: Handle tool_use stop reasons, execute functions, and return results to Claude.

sections.content:
The Tool Use Loop

When Claude decides to use a tool, stop_reason is tool_use and the response contains a tool_use content block:

// 1. Send request with tools
const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  tools,
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'What is the weather in London?' }]
});

// 2. Check for tool use
if (response.stop_reason === 'tool_use') {
  const toolBlock = response.content.find(b => b.type === 'tool_use');
  // toolBlock.name = 'get_weather', toolBlock.input = { city: 'London' }
  
  // 3. Execute your function
  const result = await getWeather(toolBlock.input);
  
  // 4. Send result back
  const final = await client.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
    max_tokens: 1024,
    tools,
    messages: [
      { role: 'user', content: 'What is the weather in London?' },
      { role: 'assistant', content: response.content },
      { role: 'user', content: [{ type: 'tool_result', tool_use_id: toolBlock.id, content: JSON.stringify(result) }] }
    ]
  });
}

exercises.question: Write a complete tool use loop that handles a single tool call and returns the final text response.
exercises.explanation: The tool loop: send message -> detect tool_use -> execute function -> send result back as tool_result -> get final response. The tool_use_id links the result to the correct call.

exercises.question: What role does the tool_result message have?
exercises.explanation: Tool results are sent as role 'user' messages with content type 'tool_result'. This matches the alternating user/assistant pattern.

exercises.question: Extend the tool loop to handle multiple sequential tool calls (Claude may need to call several tools).
exercises.explanation: The agentic loop continues until Claude returns end_turn. Each iteration may contain multiple parallel tool calls, so process all tool_use blocks in the response.

lessons.title: Tool Choice & Forced Tools
lessons.description: Control when Claude uses tools with tool_choice: auto, any, and specific tool forcing.

sections.content:
Tool Choice

By default, Claude decides on its own whether to use a tool or just respond with text. The tool_choice parameter gives you control over this decision, which is critical for building reliable pipelines where you need predictable behavior.

The Three Modes

  • { type: 'auto' } — Claude decides whether to use tools (default). Best for conversational interfaces where Claude should sometimes answer directly and sometimes call tools.
  • { type: 'any' } — Claude must use at least one tool but chooses which one. Useful when you know a tool call is needed but the specific tool depends on context.
  • { type: 'tool', name: 'get_weather' } — Claude must use this exact tool. No discretion. Use when the next action is deterministic.
const r = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  tools,
  tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: 'get_weather' },
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Tell me about London' }]
});

Forced Tools for Structured Output

One of the most powerful patterns is using forced tools to get structured data. Define a tool whose input_schema matches your desired output format, force Claude to call it, and the tool call arguments ARE your structured data. For instance, if you are building a data extraction pipeline, you can force an 'extract_entities' tool that always returns people, places, and dates in a consistent JSON shape. This is more reliable than asking Claude to output JSON in a text response.

Common Mistake

Do not force every tool call in an agent loop — this removes Claude's ability to reason about which action to take next. Use forcing only when the next step is always the same.

exercises.question: Write a structured extraction function that forces Claude to use an 'extract_entities' tool to pull people, places, and dates from text.
exercises.explanation: Forcing a tool turns Claude into a structured extractor. The tool input IS the structured output. No need to complete the loop since you only want the extracted data.

exercises.question: What does tool_choice: { type: 'any' } do?
options: Claude must use at least one tool
exercises.explanation: type 'any' forces Claude to call at least one tool but lets it choose which one. Useful when you want guaranteed tool usage without specifying which.

exercises.question: Build a router that uses tool_choice 'any' with 'classify_intent' and 'extract_data' tools, then dispatches based on which Claude chose.
exercises.explanation: Using 'any' with multiple tools lets Claude route between different processing paths. Check tb.name to dispatch accordingly.

lessons.title: Parallel Tool Calls
lessons.description: Handle Claude calling multiple tools simultaneously in a single response.

sections.content:
Parallel Tool Calls

Claude is smart enough to recognize when multiple tool calls can happen at the same time. For example, if you ask 'What is the weather in Tokyo, London, and New York?', Claude will emit three tool_use blocks in a single response rather than calling them one at a time. This is a significant performance optimization — three parallel API calls take the time of one, not three.

You must return a tool_result for every tool_use block in the response, matching each result to its call by id. If you omit a result, the API returns an error. Use Promise.all to execute all tool calls concurrently:

const toolCalls = response.content.filter(b => b.type === 'tool_use');
const results = await Promise.all(
  toolCalls.map(async tc => ({
    type: 'tool_result' as const,
    tool_use_id: tc.id,
    content: await handler(tc.name, tc.input)
  }))
);
messages.push({ role: 'user', content: results });

Error Handling in Parallel Calls

When one tool call fails in a parallel batch, you still need to return results for all of them. Set is_error: true on the failed result so Claude knows it failed and can adapt its approach. A common mistake is using Promise.all without try/catch per tool — if one tool throws, the entire batch fails and you cannot return any results. Wrap each individual call in its own try/catch instead.

exercises.question: Write a function that handles parallel tool calls using Promise.all for concurrent execution.
exercises.explanation: Promise.all runs all tool executions concurrently. Each result must include the tool_use_id to link it back to the correct call.

exercises.question: What happens if you omit a tool_result for one of the parallel tool_use blocks?
exercises.explanation: Every tool_use block must have a corresponding tool_result. Omitting one causes an API error.

exercises.question: Write a full agent loop that handles both single and parallel tool calls with error handling per tool.
exercises.explanation: Production agents need error handling per tool. Set is_error: true so Claude knows the call failed and can adapt.

lessons.title: Streaming with Tool Use
lessons.description: Stream responses that include tool calls, handling both text and tool input deltas.

sections.content:
Streaming with Tool Use

In real applications, you often want both streaming text (for responsive UI) and tool use (for actions). These work together, but there is a subtlety you need to understand: when Claude decides to call a tool, the tool input JSON arrives as a series of input_json_delta events — partial chunks of JSON that you would need to concatenate and parse yourself. Fortunately, the SDK handles this for you.

The SDK's stream.on('text') helper only fires for text deltas — it ignores tool input deltas. This means you can safely pipe text deltas to your UI without worrying about JSON fragments appearing. When the stream completes, call finalMessage() to get the fully parsed message with complete tool_use blocks ready to execute:

const stream = client.messages.stream({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  tools,
  messages
});
stream.on('text', (delta) => process.stdout.write(delta));
const msg = await stream.finalMessage();
// msg.content has fully parsed tool_use blocks

Building a Streaming Agent Loop

To build a streaming agent that shows text in real-time AND handles tool calls, combine streaming with the tool use loop. Each iteration streams text to the user, then checks if the final message contains tool calls. If it does, execute the tools, append results, and stream the next iteration. This creates the experience users expect from modern AI chat: words appearing instantly, with actions happening seamlessly in the background.

exercises.question: Write a streaming tool use handler that shows text in real-time and collects tool inputs from the final message.
exercises.explanation: stream.on('text') only fires for text deltas, not tool JSON. finalMessage() returns complete parsed tool_use blocks.

exercises.question: What event type contains partial tool input JSON during streaming?
exercises.explanation: Tool input arrives as input_json_delta events within content_block_delta. Concatenate partial_json strings to build the full input.

exercises.question: Build a complete streaming agent loop that streams text to the user and handles tool calls.
exercises.explanation: Combine streaming for real-time text with the tool loop for agentic behavior. Each iteration streams text deltas while collecting tool calls.

courses.title: Structured Output
courses.description: Get reliable JSON from Claude: prefilling, tool-based extraction, validation-retry loops, and Zod schema integration.

lessons.title: JSON Output Strategies
lessons.description: Three approaches to getting structured JSON: system prompts, prefilling, and forced tools.

sections.content:
JSON Output Strategies

Getting structured JSON from Claude is one of the most common requirements for production applications — feeding data into databases, APIs, or downstream processing. But Claude is a language model, and by default it produces natural language text. You need specific techniques to get reliable, parseable JSON every time. There are three approaches, ranging from least to most reliable.

1. System Prompt

Tell Claude to 'respond with JSON only' in the system prompt. This is the least reliable method because Claude may wrap the JSON in markdown code fences, add explanatory text before or after it, or use slightly different field names each time. It works for quick prototyping but not for production.

2. Prefilling

More reliable. Start the assistant's response with '{' using the prefilling technique. This forces Claude to continue generating a JSON object since it must build on the prefix you provide. You then prepend '{' when parsing the response. This predates the forced tool approach but still works well for simple cases.

3. Forced Tool (Recommended)

Most reliable. Define a 'dummy' tool whose input_schema matches your desired output structure. Force Claude to call this tool. The tool call arguments are your structured data, validated against the JSON Schema at the API level:

tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: 'output_json' }

This approach gives you schema validation, consistent field names, correct types, and required field enforcement — all without writing any validation code yourself. For any production system that needs structured output, this is the pattern to use.

exercises.question: Implement the forced-tool pattern to extract a product review into structured JSON (rating, summary, pros, cons).
exercises.explanation: The forced-tool pattern is the most reliable way to get structured JSON. The schema validates at the API level.

exercises.question: Which JSON extraction method is most reliable?
exercises.explanation: Forced tools validate against the JSON Schema at the API level, making them the most reliable method.

exercises.question: Write a generic extraction function that takes a JSON Schema and returns validated data using the forced-tool pattern.
exercises.explanation: A generic extractor makes the forced-tool pattern reusable. Pass any JSON Schema and get typed data back.

lessons.title: Validation-Retry Loops
lessons.description: Validate Claude's output and retry with error feedback when it doesn't match your schema.

sections.content:
Validation-Retry Loops

Forced tools guarantee that Claude's output matches your JSON Schema structure — but a structurally valid response can still be wrong. For instance, a schema says rating is a number, and Claude returns 15 when your business rule requires 1-10. Schema validation passes, but the data is still invalid. This is where validation-retry loops come in.

The pattern is simple: extract data, validate it against your business rules, and if validation fails, append the specific error to the prompt and try again. The key insight is that Claude can self-correct when you tell it exactly what went wrong. Saying 'rating must be 1-10 but you returned 15' is far more effective than a generic 'try again.'

for (let i = 0; i < maxRetries; i++) {
  const data = await extractWithTool(text + (lastError ? `\nFix: ${lastError}` : ''));
  const error = validate(data);
  if (!error) return data;
  lastError = error;
}
throw new Error('Failed after retries');

Best Practices

Always cap your retries (2-3 is usually enough). Each retry costs tokens, so unlimited retries can get expensive. Make your error messages specific and actionable — 'email field must contain an @ symbol' is better than 'invalid email.' If the same validation keeps failing after 3 retries, the problem is likely in your schema or prompt, not in Claude's output. A common pitfall is retrying on non-deterministic errors with the exact same input — if temperature is above 0, the retry might succeed by chance, but at temperature 0, you will get the same wrong answer every time unless you change the prompt.

exercises.question: Implement a validation-retry loop that extracts a date in ISO format and retries if the date is invalid.
exercises.explanation: Validation-retry feeds specific error messages back to Claude, helping it self-correct. Keep retries bounded.

exercises.question: What is the key advantage of feeding validation errors back to Claude?
options: Claude can self-correct based on specific feedback
exercises.explanation: Specific error feedback helps Claude understand what went wrong and fix it. More effective than generic retries.

exercises.question: Build a generic retry-with-validation wrapper that accepts a validator function and returns typed output.
exercises.explanation: A generic wrapper separates retry logic from validation rules. The validator returns typed data on success or an error message on failure.

lessons.title: Zod Schema Integration
lessons.description: Use Zod schemas for type-safe validation and automatic JSON Schema generation.

sections.content:
Zod Schema Integration

One of the pain points of the forced-tool approach is maintaining JSON Schema definitions by hand — they are verbose, easy to get wrong, and disconnected from your TypeScript types. Zod solves this elegantly. You define your schema once using Zod's chainable API, and it gives you three things: TypeScript types (via z.infer), runtime validation (via .parse()), and JSON Schema (via zod-to-json-schema). This means one schema definition serves your tool definitions, your response validation, and your type system.

import { z } from 'zod';
import { zodToJsonSchema } from 'zod-to-json-schema';

const PersonSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1),
  age: z.number().int().min(0).max(150),
  email: z.string().email().optional()
});

type Person = z.infer<typeof PersonSchema>;

const tool: Anthropic.Tool = {
  name: 'output_person',
  description: 'Output person data.',
  input_schema: zodToJsonSchema(PersonSchema) as any
};

Why This Matters

Without Zod, you would define a JSON Schema for the tool, a TypeScript interface for your code, and write custom validation logic — three things that can drift apart. With Zod, they stay in sync automatically. When you change the schema (say, adding a phone field), the type, the tool definition, and the validation all update together.

Zod + Retry Loops

Zod's .parse() method throws a ZodError when validation fails. This error includes detailed information for each failing field: the path, the expected type, and a human-readable message. You can format these into specific feedback that Claude can use to self-correct in a retry loop, making the combination of Zod + forced tools + retry the gold standard for structured extraction.

exercises.question: Create a Zod schema for a blog post (title, content, tags array, publishDate), convert to JSON Schema for a tool, and validate the response.
exercises.explanation: Zod gives you one schema for types, tool definitions, and validation. zodToJsonSchema converts it for the API.

exercises.question: What does Zod's .parse() do when validation fails?
options: Throws a ZodError with field-level details
exercises.explanation: ZodError contains detailed issues for each failing field, including path, expected type, and message. Perfect for feeding back to Claude in a retry loop.

exercises.question: Combine Zod validation with a retry loop: on ZodError, format the issues and feed them back to Claude.
exercises.explanation: ZodError.issues provides path and message for each field. Formatting these into a clear error message helps Claude fix exactly the right fields.

lessons.title: Complex Nested Schemas
lessons.description: Handle arrays, nested objects, discriminated unions, and recursive structures.

sections.content:
Complex Nested Schemas

Real-world data is rarely flat. Invoices have line items. User profiles have addresses with nested fields. API responses have arrays of objects. Zod handles nested structures naturally — you compose schemas just like you compose TypeScript types.

const InvoiceSchema = z.object({
  vendor: z.object({ name: z.string(), address: z.string() }),
  items: z.array(z.object({
    description: z.string(),
    quantity: z.number().int().positive(),
    unitPrice: z.number().positive()
  })),
  total: z.number()
});

When Complexity Hurts Accuracy

There is a practical limit to schema complexity. When a schema has deeply nested objects with many required fields, Claude's extraction accuracy can drop — it may miss fields, confuse nested levels, or hallucinate values. If you notice quality degrading with a complex schema, the fix is to split the extraction into multiple focused passes. For instance, extract the vendor info in one call and the line items in another, then combine the results. Each pass uses a simpler schema, which improves accuracy.

Design Principles

Extract sub-schemas into named constants for reuse (a AddressSchema used in both vendor and shipping). Use z.array() with .min(1) when at least one item is expected. For discriminated unions (data that can be one of several shapes), Zod's z.discriminatedUnion() maps cleanly to JSON Schema's oneOf. Keep individual object schemas under 10-15 properties — beyond that, consider splitting.

exercises.question: Define a Zod schema for an API error response with nested error details (code, message, field-level errors array).
exercises.starter_code:
import { z } from 'zod';

// TODO: Define ErrorResponse schema with nested structure
exercises.explanation: Nested Zod schemas compose naturally. Extract sub-schemas into named constants for reuse and readability.

exercises.question: When should you split a complex extraction into multiple API calls?
options: When extraction quality drops with deeply nested schemas
Only when the schema has more than 3 fields
exercises.explanation: Complex nested schemas can reduce extraction accuracy. Split into focused passes and combine results.

exercises.question: Build a two-pass extraction: first extract line items from an invoice, then extract totals. Combine into a final object.
exercises.explanation: Multi-pass extraction runs focused extractions in parallel. Each pass handles a simpler schema, improving accuracy. Promise.all keeps latency low.

lessons.title: Extraction Pipelines
lessons.description: Build production extraction pipelines that chain classification, extraction, and enrichment.

sections.content:
Extraction Pipelines

In production, extraction is rarely a single step. Real documents come in different formats, contain different types of data, and need different levels of validation. Extraction pipelines chain multiple stages, each doing one thing well: classify (Haiku, fast) -> extract (Sonnet, accurate) -> validate (Zod) -> enrich (external APIs).

Model Routing for Cost Efficiency

A key insight is that different stages need different models. Classification is simple — Haiku can tell you if a document is an invoice, a contract, or a receipt for a fraction of a cent. Extraction requires more reasoning — Sonnet's accuracy justifies the higher cost. This classify-then-extract pattern can cut your API costs by 60-80% compared to using Sonnet for everything.

async function pipeline(doc: string) {
  const { type } = await classify(doc);           // Haiku
  const schema = schemas[type];                    // pick schema
  const data = await extract(doc, schema);         // Sonnet
  const valid = await validate(data, schema);      // Zod
  return valid;
}

Batch Processing

When you have thousands of documents to process, run classifications concurrently with a concurrency limiter (to avoid rate limits), then batch extractions. Use Promise.all with a worker pool pattern rather than sequential processing. For non-urgent workloads, consider the Batch API for an additional 50% cost savings. A common pitfall is not handling partial failures — if 1 out of 100 extractions fails, you should retry that one item rather than reprocessing the entire batch.

exercises.question: Build a classify-then-extract pipeline: classify a support ticket as 'bug', 'feature', or 'question', then extract type-specific fields.
exercises.explanation: Classify with Haiku (cheap, fast), extract with Sonnet (accurate). Different schemas per type improve accuracy.

exercises.question: Why use Haiku for classification and Sonnet for extraction?
options: Haiku is cheaper for simple tasks; Sonnet is more accurate for complex extraction
exercises.explanation: Classification is simple enough for Haiku at low cost. Extraction requires more reasoning where Sonnet's accuracy justifies higher cost.

exercises.question: Build a batch extraction pipeline that processes an array of documents concurrently with a concurrency limit.
exercises.explanation: A worker pool limits concurrent API calls to avoid rate limits while maximizing throughput.

courses.title: The Agent SDK
courses.description: Build autonomous agents with the Claude Agent SDK: define agents, run loops, use subagents, and implement guardrails.

lessons.title: Agent Definitions & the Run Loop
lessons.description: Define agents with instructions, tools, and model config. Understand the run loop lifecycle.

sections.content:
Agent Definitions

Up to this point, you have been writing the tool loop yourself — sending messages, checking for tool_use, executing functions, feeding results back. The Claude Agent SDK abstracts all of this into a declarative pattern where you define WHAT an agent can do, and the SDK handles HOW the loop runs.

An agent is defined by three things: its instructions (what role it plays and how it should behave), its tools (what actions it can take), and its model (the capability level). You then call run() to start the autonomous loop:

import { Agent, run } from '@anthropic-ai/agent-sdk';

const agent = new Agent({
  name: 'researcher',
  instructions: 'You research topics and return summaries.',
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  tools: [webSearchTool, readUrlTool]
});

const result = await run(agent, 'Research quantum computing');
console.log(result.output);

The Run Loop

When you call run(), the SDK enters an autonomous loop: Claude receives the prompt, decides whether to use a tool or respond directly, tools execute and results feed back, and the cycle continues until Claude produces a final response (no more tool calls). This is the same loop you built by hand in the Tool Use course, but now it is managed for you.

Why Declarative Agents Matter

The declarative approach lets you focus on agent design rather than plumbing. You can experiment with different instructions, swap tools in and out, and change models — all without rewriting loop logic. It also makes agents composable: one agent can hand off to another, creating multi-agent systems (covered in the next course). A common mistake is writing instructions that are too vague. Good instructions tell the agent what approach to take ('search for context first, then read relevant files') rather than just stating the goal.

exercises.question: Define an agent that has a calculator tool and can solve math word problems.
exercises.explanation: The Agent SDK's tool() helper wraps your function with schema validation. The agent autonomously decides when to call the calculator.

exercises.question: What happens when run() is called on an agent?
options: An autonomous loop until final response
exercises.explanation: run() enters an autonomous loop: prompt -> Claude decides -> tools execute -> results feed back -> repeat until final response.

exercises.question: Define an agent with multiple tools (search, read_file, write_file) and instructions that guide when to use each.
exercises.explanation: Good agent instructions guide tool selection. Phrases like 'search first' and 'verify before writing' create reliable patterns.

lessons.title: Tool Definitions in the Agent SDK
lessons.description: Define tools with Zod schemas, async execution, and error handling.

sections.content:
Agent SDK Tools

In the raw Messages API, you define tools as JSON Schema objects and handle tool execution yourself. The Agent SDK's tool() helper improves this in three important ways: you use Zod schemas instead of raw JSON Schema (type-safe, composable), the execute function receives validated input (no need to check types yourself), and errors are automatically caught and fed back to Claude as tool errors so the agent can adapt its approach.

const dbQuery = tool({
  name: 'query_database',
  description: 'Run a read-only SQL query.',
  schema: z.object({ sql: z.string(), params: z.array(z.string()).optional() }),
  async execute({ sql, params }) {
    const rows = await db.query(sql, params);
    return JSON.stringify(rows);
  }
});

Error Handling Strategy

There are two kinds of errors in tools: expected failures and unexpected failures. For expected failures (file not found, invalid input, API returned 404), return an error message as a string — the agent sees it and tries a different approach. For unexpected failures (network timeout, bug in your code), let the error throw — the SDK catches it and sends it to Claude as a tool error. A common pitfall is catching all errors and returning empty strings. This hides failures from the agent, which then proceeds with bad data. Always give the agent enough information to understand what went wrong and decide its next step.

Tool Design Tips

Keep tools focused — one tool should do one thing. Write descriptions as if explaining the tool to a colleague who has never seen your codebase. Include what the tool returns, not just what it does. If two tools overlap in purpose, add explicit disambiguation ('Use this for internal docs; use search_web for external content').

exercises.question: Define a tool that calls an external API with proper error handling.
exercises.explanation: Return error messages as strings for expected failures so Claude can adapt. Only throw for unexpected errors.

exercises.question: What happens when a tool's execute() throws an error in the Agent SDK?
options: The error is sent to Claude as a tool error so it can adapt
exercises.explanation: The SDK catches thrown errors and sends them back as tool errors. Claude can then retry or take an alternative approach.

exercises.question: Create a tool that validates its input beyond the Zod schema (e.g., checks that a date is in the future).
exercises.explanation: Zod handles type validation, but business rules need manual checks inside execute(). Return clear messages so Claude can self-correct.

lessons.title: Subagents & Handoffs
lessons.description: Delegate tasks to specialized subagents and transfer control between agents.

sections.content:
Subagents & Handoffs

A single agent trying to do everything — write SQL, generate charts, send emails, manage files — tends to perform poorly. Its instructions get long and contradictory, and it struggles to decide which tool to use. The solution is to split responsibilities across specialized agents and let them hand off work to each other.

The Agent SDK supports this natively through the handoffs property. When you list agents in another agent's handoffs array, the parent agent can transfer control to any of them. The subagent runs its own independent loop with its own tools and instructions, and when it finishes, its output returns to the parent agent to continue:

const sqlAgent = new Agent({
  name: 'sql_expert',
  instructions: 'Write optimized SQL queries.',
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  tools: [queryTool]
});

const analyst = new Agent({
  name: 'data_analyst',
  instructions: 'Analyze data by delegating SQL to the sql_expert.',
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  tools: [chartTool],
  handoffs: [sqlAgent]
});

When to Use Handoffs

Handoffs are ideal when subtasks require different expertise, different tools, or different levels of trust. For instance, a triage agent using Haiku (cheap, fast) can route customer inquiries to a billing specialist or a technical specialist, each with their own domain-specific tools. This mirrors how real support teams work. A common pitfall is creating too many handoffs — if an agent has 10 possible handoffs, it may struggle to pick the right one. Keep handoff options focused and distinct, with clear descriptions on each subagent so the parent knows when to delegate.

exercises.question: Create a research agent that hands off to a fact_checker subagent to verify claims.
exercises.explanation: Handoffs create quality gates. The researcher searches, the fact checker verifies. Each agent focuses on what it does best.

exercises.question: What happens when an agent hands off to a subagent?
options: The subagent runs its own loop and returns output to the parent
exercises.explanation: The subagent runs independently. When it finishes, its output returns to the parent which continues its own loop.

exercises.question: Build a customer service system with a triage agent that hands off to billing_agent or technical_agent.
exercises.explanation: Triage uses Haiku (cheap, fast) to route. Specialists use Sonnet with domain-specific tools. Mirrors real support team structure.

lessons.title: Guardrails & Output Validation
lessons.description: Add safety guardrails, output validators, and execution limits to agents.

sections.content:
Guardrails

An agent without guardrails is a liability. It can loop forever (burning money), follow malicious instructions injected in user input (prompt injection), or leak sensitive data in its responses (PII exposure). Production agents need three layers of protection: input guardrails, output guardrails, and execution limits.

Input Guardrails

Before the agent sees user input, screen it for prompt injection attempts. A fast, cheap approach: use Haiku to classify whether the input looks like a normal request or an injection attempt. This adds milliseconds and fractions of a cent per request — negligible compared to the risk of a compromised agent. Block or flag suspicious inputs before they reach your main agent.

Output Guardrails

After the agent produces a response, scan it for sensitive data that should not leave your system. Regex patterns catch common PII formats (emails, phone numbers, SSNs, credit card numbers). For production systems, combine regex with a dedicated PII detection service for higher accuracy.

Execution Limits

Always set maxTurns to prevent infinite loops. Without it, an agent that keeps calling tools without making progress can burn through your API budget in minutes:

const agent = new Agent({
  name: 'assistant',
  instructions: 'Help users.',
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  tools: [searchTool],
  maxTurns: 10
});

A reasonable maxTurns depends on the task: 5-10 for simple tasks, 20-30 for complex multi-step workflows. If your agent consistently hits the limit, the problem is usually in the instructions or tool design, not the limit itself.

exercises.question: Implement an input guardrail that uses Haiku to detect prompt injection attempts.
exercises.explanation: Input guardrails run before the main agent. Haiku is cheap enough to screen every request.

exercises.question: Why should you always set maxTurns on an agent?
options: To prevent infinite loops and runaway costs
exercises.explanation: Without maxTurns, an agent could loop indefinitely, wasting tokens and money.

exercises.question: Build an output guardrail that detects PII (emails, phones, SSNs) and redacts them.
exercises.explanation: PII redaction is a critical output guardrail. Regex catches common patterns. For production, combine with a dedicated PII detection service.

lessons.title: Agent Memory & Context Management
lessons.description: Manage context windows, implement memory strategies, and handle long-running sessions.

sections.content:
Memory & Context

Every turn in an agent loop adds messages to the conversation history: the user's input, Claude's response, tool calls, tool results. Over a long session, this history grows until it hits the context window limit. At that point, the agent either fails or starts losing important earlier context. Managing memory is essential for agents that run extended sessions or process large amounts of data.

Four Memory Strategies

  • Full history — Send everything every turn. Simple to implement, but you hit the context window limit fast. Fine for short sessions (under 20 turns).
  • Sliding window — Keep only the last N turns. Easy to implement and keeps token usage constant, but earlier context is lost entirely. The agent forgets what happened 30 minutes ago.
  • Summarization — Compress older messages into a compact summary, keep recent messages intact. Preserves the most information within token limits. Use a cheap model (Haiku) to generate summaries to keep costs low.
  • RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — Store facts and decisions in an external database (vector store, key-value store). Retrieve relevant context per turn based on the current query. Best for very long sessions or persistent agents that need to remember across conversations.

Choosing the Right Strategy

Start with full history. When you hit context limits, upgrade to sliding window for simplicity or summarization for quality. Use RAG when the agent needs long-term memory across sessions. A common pitfall is summarizing too aggressively — if the summary drops critical details (like a user's name or a specific requirement stated early in the conversation), the agent's quality degrades. Include a 'key facts' section in your summaries to preserve critical information.

exercises.question: Implement a sliding window memory manager that keeps the last N message pairs.
exercises.explanation: Sliding window preserves recent context while staying within limits. Trade-off: older context is lost entirely.

exercises.question: Which memory strategy preserves the most context within token limits?
options: Summarization of old messages + recent history
exercises.explanation: Summarization compresses old context while keeping recent messages intact. Preserves key facts without hitting token limits.

exercises.question: Build a summarization memory manager that uses Claude to compress old messages.
exercises.explanation: Use cheap Haiku to summarize. The summary becomes a synthetic message pair. Recent messages stay intact.

courses.title: Multi-Agent Architectures
courses.description: Design multi-agent systems: coordinator-worker patterns, pipelines, parallel fan-out, and agent communication.

lessons.title: Coordinator-Worker Pattern
lessons.description: A coordinator decomposes tasks and delegates to specialized workers.

sections.content:
Coordinator-Worker

The coordinator-worker pattern is the most important multi-agent architecture. A coordinator agent receives a complex task, decomposes it into subtasks, and delegates each to a specialized worker. Each worker has its own focused instructions, tools, and model — optimized for its specific role. The coordinator then aggregates results and handles any failures.

This pattern mirrors real teams: a project manager decomposes work, specialists execute, and the manager synthesizes the outcome. Each specialist is better at their narrow task than a generalist would be, which is exactly why this pattern produces higher quality output than a single omniscient agent.

const writer = new Agent({ name: 'writer', instructions: 'Write content.', model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514' });
const reviewer = new Agent({ name: 'reviewer', instructions: 'Review for quality.', model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514' });

const coordinator = new Agent({
  name: 'coordinator',
  instructions: 'Break tasks into writing and reviewing. Delegate accordingly.',
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  handoffs: [writer, reviewer]
});

Design Decisions

The coordinator's instructions are the most important: they determine how tasks get decomposed. Be explicit about which worker handles what ('delegate research to the researcher, writing to the writer'). Choose models strategically — the coordinator can use Haiku for cheap routing decisions, while workers use Sonnet for quality output. A common pitfall is making the coordinator do work itself rather than delegating. Keep the coordinator focused on orchestration.

exercises.question: Build a coordinator-worker system for a blog pipeline: researcher, writer, editor.
exercises.explanation: Each agent has a focused role. The coordinator orchestrates the sequence. This produces higher quality than a single agent.

exercises.question: What is the main advantage of coordinator-worker over a single agent?
options: Specialized agents produce better results per task
exercises.explanation: Specialized agents with focused instructions outperform a single generalist. Each worker can be tuned for its role.

exercises.question: Add error handling: if a worker fails, retry once, then use a fallback.
exercises.explanation: Multi-agent systems need resilience. Retry once, then fallback to prevent the whole pipeline from crashing.

lessons.title: Pipeline Architecture
lessons.description: Chain agents sequentially where each agent's output feeds the next.

sections.content:
Pipeline Architecture

A pipeline is the simplest multi-agent pattern: agents are chained sequentially, where each agent's output becomes the next agent's input. Unlike the coordinator-worker pattern where the coordinator makes decisions about delegation, a pipeline has a fixed, predetermined flow. Data enters at stage one and flows through every stage in order.

async function pipeline(input: string) {
  const r1 = await run(extractAgent, input);
  const r2 = await run(transformAgent, r1.output);
  const r3 = await run(loadAgent, r2.output);
  return r3.output;
}

When to Use Pipelines

Pipelines excel when the processing steps are well-defined, always happen in the same order, and each step has a clear input/output contract. Classic examples include ETL (extract, transform, load), content processing (draft, edit, format), code review (read, analyze, report), and document conversion (parse, restructure, validate). Each agent is focused, testable, and replaceable.

Pipeline vs. Coordinator

If your workflow sometimes skips steps, reorders steps, or needs conditional branching, use a coordinator instead — it has the intelligence to make routing decisions. Pipelines are rigid by design, which is their strength for predictable workflows and their weakness for dynamic ones. A common pitfall is building a pipeline when you actually need a coordinator, leading to wasted stages that produce empty output on certain inputs. Another pitfall is not adding short-circuit logic — if stage 1 classifies content as 'safe,' there is no reason to run the remaining stages in a moderation pipeline.

exercises.question: Build a content moderation pipeline: classify -> extract_violations -> decide_action.
exercises.explanation: The pipeline short-circuits on safe content. Only suspicious content goes through expensive stages.

exercises.question: When is a pipeline preferred over coordinator-worker?
options: When data flows sequentially with clear input/output contracts
exercises.explanation: Pipelines excel with sequential data transformations. Coordinator-worker is better when subtasks are independent.

exercises.question: Create a generic pipeline runner that chains agents with logging between stages.
exercises.explanation: A generic runner makes composing agents easy. The onStage callback enables logging and monitoring.

lessons.title: Parallel Fan-Out
lessons.description: Run multiple agents in parallel and merge results.

sections.content:
Parallel Fan-Out

When subtasks are independent — meaning no agent needs another agent's output to do its work — you can run them in parallel using Promise.all. This is a massive latency optimization: if three agents each take 5 seconds, running them in parallel takes 5 seconds total instead of 15. The pattern has two phases: fan-out (run agents concurrently) and merge (combine results with a synthesizer agent).

const [research, competitors, market] = await Promise.all([
  run(researchAgent, topic),
  run(competitorAgent, topic),
  run(marketAgent, topic)
]);
const synthesis = await run(synthesizer, 
  `Research: ${research.output}\nCompetitors: ${competitors.output}\nMarket: ${market.output}`);

Fan-Out Best Practices

The synthesizer agent is critical — it combines perspectives and resolves contradictions. Give it clear instructions about how to weight and merge the inputs. Use Promise.allSettled instead of Promise.all when partial results are acceptable. With Promise.all, one failure cancels everything. With Promise.allSettled, you get results from all successful agents and error information from failed ones. A common pitfall is fanning out too aggressively — running 50 agents simultaneously will hit rate limits and exhaust system resources. Use a concurrency limiter (5-10 concurrent agents) for larger fan-outs.

exercises.question: Build a fan-out that analyzes a product from 3 angles in parallel, then synthesizes.
exercises.explanation: Fan-out runs 3 analyses simultaneously. Latency = slowest agent, not 3x. The synthesizer combines perspectives.

exercises.question: Total latency of a fan-out with agents taking 2s, 3s, and 5s?
exercises.explanation: Promise.all runs concurrently. Total time = slowest agent (5s), not sum (10s).

exercises.question: Build a generic fan-out-merge that handles partial failures gracefully.
exercises.explanation: Promise.allSettled collects both successes and failures. Merge only successful results. Report failures for the caller to handle.

lessons.title: Agent Communication Patterns
lessons.description: Agents sharing context via message passing, shared state, and events.

sections.content:
Agent Communication

In multi-agent systems, agents need to share information. How they communicate determines the system's reliability, debuggability, and scalability. There are three primary patterns, each suited to different architectures.

1. Message Passing

The output of one agent becomes the input of another. This is the simplest and most debuggable pattern — you can inspect exactly what data flows between agents. Pipelines and coordinator-worker systems use message passing. The coordinator decides what context to pass to each worker, keeping communication explicit and traceable. Best for: sequential workflows, hierarchical systems.

2. Shared State

Agents read and write a shared store (in-memory map, database, file system) via tools. This enables collaboration without direct agent-to-agent communication. For instance, a research agent writes findings to a shared store, and a writing agent reads them when generating content. The danger is race conditions — if two agents write to the same key simultaneously, one overwrites the other. Use coordination (locks or sequential access) for shared resources. Best for: collaborative tasks where multiple agents contribute to the same artifact.

3. Event System

Agents emit events that others subscribe to and react to in real-time. This is the most decoupled pattern — agents do not need to know about each other, only about events. Useful for monitoring (a logging agent subscribes to all tool-call events), dynamic workflows (a new agent spins up when a specific event fires), and reactive systems. The trade-off is complexity — event-driven systems are harder to debug than message passing. Best for: real-time monitoring, loosely coupled systems.

exercises.question: Implement a shared state store that agents can read/write via tools.
exercises.explanation: Shared state via tools lets agents collaborate. Each agent reads/writes through tools, maintaining consistency.

exercises.question: Which pattern is best for agents reacting to each other in real-time?
exercises.explanation: An event system lets agents emit and subscribe to events in real-time for dynamic workflows.

exercises.question: Build a simple event emitter that agents can use to broadcast findings.
exercises.explanation: The event bus decouples agents. Subscribers react to events outside the agent loop.

lessons.title: Evaluating Multi-Agent Systems
lessons.description: Test, benchmark, and debug multi-agent systems.

sections.content:
Evaluating Multi-Agent Systems

Multi-agent systems are harder to test than single-agent systems because failures can happen at any boundary: within an agent, between agents, in tool execution, or in the coordination logic. A systematic evaluation strategy covers three levels.

Unit Testing Agents

Test each agent in isolation with mock tools that return predetermined responses. This lets you verify that the agent calls the right tools in the right order, handles errors gracefully, and produces correct output for known inputs. Mock tools make tests fast, deterministic, and free from API costs.

Integration Testing

Run the full multi-agent system end-to-end with representative inputs. Check that agents hand off correctly, that context flows between stages without loss, and that the final output is correct. Integration tests are slower and cost real API tokens, so run them less frequently — nightly or before deployments.

Key Metrics

Track four metrics in production: task completion rate (does the system solve the problem?), token usage per task (is cost predictable?), latency (p50 and p95, are users waiting too long?), and error recovery rate (when a tool fails, does the agent recover?). Task completion rate is the most important — optimizing speed or cost at the expense of correctness is always a mistake. Dashboard these metrics and alert on regressions.

exercises.question: Write a test harness that records tool calls and verifies the sequence.
exercises.explanation: A test harness records tool calls for assertion. Mock responses create deterministic environments for testing.

exercises.question: What is the most important metric for evaluating a multi-agent system?
exercises.explanation: Task completion rate measures whether the system solves the problem. Other optimizations are secondary to correctness.

exercises.question: Build a token usage tracker across all agents in a multi-agent run.
exercises.explanation: Token tracking reveals which agents are most expensive, guiding optimization decisions.

courses.title: MCP Integration
courses.description: Build and consume Model Context Protocol servers: expose tools, resources, and prompts to any MCP client.

lessons.title: MCP Fundamentals
lessons.description: Understand the Model Context Protocol: servers, clients, transports, capabilities.

sections.content:
MCP Fundamentals

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that provides a universal way to connect AI models to external tools and data sources. Before MCP, every integration was custom: if you wanted Claude to query your database, you wrote bespoke tool handlers. If you switched to a different AI client, you rewrote everything. MCP standardizes this connection, so a tool built once works with any MCP-compatible client — Claude Code, Claude Desktop, or any third-party application.

Architecture

  • Server — A process that exposes tools, resources, and prompts via the MCP protocol. You build these to wrap your APIs, databases, and internal systems.
  • Client — Claude Desktop, Claude Code, or any application that discovers and calls MCP tools. The client does not need to know how the server works internally.
  • Transport — How client and server communicate. stdio for local servers (spawned as child processes), SSE/HTTP for remote servers accessible over the network.

Servers expose three capabilities: Tools (callable functions that perform actions), Resources (read-only data the client can browse), and Prompts (reusable prompt templates). Most servers start with tools and add resources and prompts as needed.

import { McpServer } from '@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js';
const server = new McpServer({ name: 'my-server', version: '1.0.0' });

exercises.question: What are the three capabilities an MCP server can expose?
exercises.explanation: MCP servers expose tools (functions), resources (data), and prompts (templates).

exercises.question: Create a basic MCP server with a 'hello' tool.
exercises.explanation: MCP tools register with name, Zod schema, and handler. StdioServerTransport connects via stdin/stdout.

exercises.question: Which transport is used for local MCP servers?
exercises.explanation: stdio transport uses stdin/stdout for local servers spawned as child processes.

lessons.title: Building MCP Tools
lessons.description: Register tools with schemas, handle errors, return structured results.

sections.content:
Building MCP Tools

MCP tools follow a simple but important pattern: register a tool with a name, a Zod schema for parameters, and an async handler function. The handler receives validated input and must return a result object with a content array. When things go wrong, return the error in the content with isError: true — never throw unhandled exceptions, as they crash the server and disconnect all clients.

server.tool('query_db', { sql: z.string() }, async ({ sql }) => {
  try {
    const rows = await db.query(sql);
    return { content: [{ type: 'text', text: JSON.stringify(rows) }] };
  } catch (e) {
    return { content: [{ type: 'text', text: e.message }], isError: true };
  }
});

Error Handling Matters

The isError: true flag tells the client that the tool call failed, so Claude can adapt its approach (retry with different parameters, try an alternative tool, or report the issue to the user). Without it, Claude treats error messages as successful results and may incorporate error text into its response. Always wrap your handler body in try/catch and return structured error messages.

Tool Design for MCP

Group related tools on one server (a 'filesystem' server with read, write, and list tools). Keep tool responses concise — returning a 10MB database dump overwhelms the context window. Paginate large results. Add descriptions to both the tool and every parameter. Good MCP tools are discoverable: a client seeing only the tool name and description should understand what it does without reading the source code.

exercises.question: Build an MCP tool that reads a file with error handling for missing files.
exercises.explanation: MCP tools should never throw unhandled errors. Return isError content so clients get helpful messages.

exercises.question: How should MCP tools report errors?
exercises.explanation: Return errors as content with isError: true so clients and Claude understand what went wrong.

exercises.question: Register multiple related tools (list_dir, read_file, write_file) on one server.
exercises.explanation: Group related tools on one server with consistent error handling.

lessons.title: MCP Resources
lessons.description: Expose data as browsable, readable resources.

sections.content:
MCP Resources

While tools perform actions (query a database, send an email, write a file), resources provide read-only data that clients can browse and load into context. Think of resources as a data catalog: the client can list available resources, read their contents, and use that data to inform its actions. Resources are ideal for configuration files, documentation pages, database records, and any data that should be readable but not modifiable through MCP.

Static vs. Dynamic Resources

Static resources have a fixed URI (like config://app) and always return the same structure. Dynamic resources use URI templates with placeholders (like users://{userId}/profile) that the client fills in. Dynamic resources are powerful for exposing database records, user profiles, or any entity-based data:

server.resource('config', 'config://app', async (uri) => ({
  contents: [{ uri: uri.href, mimeType: 'application/json', text: JSON.stringify(config) }]
}));

// Dynamic with templates
server.resource('user', 'users://{userId}/profile', async (uri, { userId }) => {
  const profile = await db.getUser(userId);
  return { contents: [{ uri: uri.href, mimeType: 'application/json', text: JSON.stringify(profile) }] };
});

Security Consideration

Resources should never expose secrets or sensitive data. Filter out API keys, passwords, and tokens before returning content. Since resources are read-only by design, they provide a safer way to give Claude access to your data compared to a generic 'read anything' tool.

exercises.question: Register a resource that serves environment config, filtering out secrets.
exercises.explanation: Resources should never expose secrets. Filter by key name patterns.

exercises.question: Difference between MCP tools and resources?
options: Tools perform actions; resources provide read-only data
exercises.explanation: Tools are callable functions that perform actions. Resources provide read-only data.

exercises.question: Create a dynamic resource template that serves database records by table and ID.
exercises.explanation: URI templates with {placeholders} create dynamic resources. The client resolves parameters.

lessons.title: MCP Prompts
lessons.description: Define reusable prompt templates that clients can discover and invoke.

sections.content:
MCP Prompts

MCP prompts are reusable prompt templates that encapsulate best practices for common tasks. Instead of each user writing their own 'review this code' prompt, you define it once on the server with the optimal structure, and any client can discover and use it. This standardizes how your team interacts with Claude across different tools and contexts.

server.prompt('code-review', {
  language: z.string(),
  code: z.string()
}, ({ language, code }) => ({
  messages: [{
    role: 'user',
    content: { type: 'text', text: `Review this ${language} code:\n\n${code}` }
  }]
}));

When to Use MCP Prompts

Prompts shine when you have standardized workflows that multiple people or tools need to invoke consistently. Code review prompts, documentation generation templates, bug report formats, security audit checklists — anything where the prompt structure is more important than letting each user write their own. The client discovers available prompts via the MCP protocol and renders them with user-provided parameters.

Prompts vs. Tools

A common source of confusion: tools perform actions and return results within the conversation. Prompts define how a conversation starts — they generate the initial messages that the client sends to Claude. Think of prompts as conversation starters and tools as mid-conversation actions. In practice, most MCP servers focus on tools first and add prompts later for common interaction patterns.

exercises.question: Register a 'summarize' prompt template with content and optional length parameter.
exercises.explanation: MCP prompts standardize common interactions. Clients discover and render them with parameters.

exercises.question: What is the purpose of MCP prompts?
options: Reusable templates clients can discover and invoke
exercises.explanation: MCP prompts standardize common workflows across different clients.

exercises.question: Build a complete MCP server with tools, resources, and prompts for a docs system.
exercises.explanation: A complete server combines all three capabilities: search tool, page resource, explain prompt.

lessons.title: Connecting MCP to Claude
lessons.description: Configure Claude Desktop and Claude Code to use MCP servers.

sections.content:
Connecting MCP to Claude

Building an MCP server is only half the job — you also need to configure your client (Claude Code or Claude Desktop) to discover and connect to it. The configuration tells the client how to start your server, what environment variables to pass, and where to find it.

Claude Code Configuration

Add a .mcp.json file in your project root for project-specific servers. The config specifies the command to start the server, arguments, and environment variables:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "my-server": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["./server.js"],
      "env": { "DB_URL": "..." }
    }
  }
}

For servers you want available across all projects, use the global config at ~/.claude/.mcp.json. Use the /mcp command in Claude Code to verify your servers are connected and list their available tools.

Environment Variables for Secrets

Pass API keys and database credentials through the env field rather than hardcoding them in the server code. This keeps secrets out of your source repository and lets different environments (development, staging, production) use different credentials. The server reads these from process.env at startup.

Debugging Connections

If a server fails to connect, check three things: the command path is correct (try running it manually), the server starts without errors (check stderr output), and the server responds to the MCP initialization handshake. The /mcp command in Claude Code shows connection status and any errors for each configured server.

exercises.question: Write a .mcp.json config connecting a database server and file system server with env vars.
exercises.starter_code: // TODO: JSON config for two MCP servers
exercises.explanation: Each server gets command, args, and env. Env vars pass config without hardcoding secrets.

exercises.question: Where should project-specific MCP configs go for Claude Code?
exercises.explanation: .mcp.json in project root for project-specific servers. ~/.claude/.mcp.json for global servers.

exercises.question: Write a health-check that verifies an MCP server starts and lists its tools.
exercises.explanation: Health checks verify servers start and respond to protocol requests. Use in CI/CD or startup scripts.

courses.title: Production Patterns
courses.description: Ship reliable AI features: error handling, retries, Batch API, rate limiting, caching, and cost controls.

lessons.title: Error Handling & Retries
lessons.description: Handle API errors with exponential backoff and circuit breakers.

sections.content:
Error Handling

When you move from prototyping to production, error handling becomes the difference between a reliable system and one that crashes at 2 AM. The Anthropic API returns specific HTTP status codes that tell you exactly what went wrong and whether you should retry. Understanding these codes is essential for building resilient applications.

Key error codes: 400 (bad request — your input is malformed, fix the request, do not retry), 401 (authentication failed — check your API key, do not retry), 429 (rate limited — you are sending too many requests, wait and retry), 500 (server error — temporary issue on Anthropic's side, retry with backoff), 529 (overloaded — the API is at capacity, retry with longer backoff). The critical insight: 400 and 401 errors are your fault and retrying will not help. 429, 500, and 529 are transient and will resolve if you wait.

async function withRetry(fn: () => Promise, max = 3): Promise {
  for (let i = 0; i <= max; i++) {
    try { return await fn(); }
    catch (e: any) {
      if (i === max || e.status === 400 || e.status === 401) throw e;
      const delay = Math.min(1000 * Math.pow(2, i) + Math.random() * 1000, 30000);
      await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, delay));
    }
  }
  throw new Error('Unreachable');
}

exercises.question: Implement exponential backoff that respects Retry-After headers.
exercises.explanation: Respect Retry-After headers. Add jitter to prevent thundering herd. Cap maximum delay.

exercises.question: Which HTTP status codes should you retry?
exercises.explanation: Retry 429 (rate limited), 500 (server error), 529 (overloaded). Never retry 400 or 401.

exercises.question: Build a circuit breaker that stops API calls after N consecutive failures.
exercises.explanation: After N failures, the circuit opens and blocks calls for a cooldown period. Protects during outages.

lessons.title: The Batch API
lessons.description: Process thousands of requests at 50% cost with Message Batches.

sections.content:
Batch API

The Message Batches API is one of the most powerful cost optimization tools available. Instead of sending requests one at a time and paying full price, you submit a batch of up to 100,000 requests and pay 50% of the standard price. The trade-off: results are not instant — they are processed within a 24-hour window. This makes batch ideal for any workload that is not time-sensitive: nightly code analysis, bulk content classification, weekly report generation, training data preparation, or large-scale document processing.

Each request in the batch gets a custom_id that you define. When results come back, you match them to your original requests by this ID. Use structured IDs like doc_invoice-2024-001 so you can parse and route results programmatically. Process up to 100K requests at 50% pricing, with results within 24 hours:

const batch = await client.messages.batches.create({
  requests: items.map((item, i) => ({
    custom_id: `item-${i}`,
    params: { model: 'claude-haiku-3-5-20241022', max_tokens: 256, messages: [{ role: 'user', content: item }] }
  }))
});
// Poll until ended, then stream results

exercises.question: Submit a batch of classification tasks and poll until completion.
exercises.explanation: Batch API: 50% cost, up to 100K requests, results within 24h. Poll processing_status until 'ended'.

exercises.question: Cost advantage of the Batch API?
exercises.explanation: 50% of standard pricing. Trade-off: results within 24 hours instead of seconds.

exercises.question: Build a batch processor that handles partial failures and retries failed items.
exercises.explanation: Track successes, re-batch only failures. Cap retries to prevent infinite loops.

lessons.title: Rate Limiting & Concurrency
lessons.description: Manage API rate limits with request queues and token buckets.

sections.content:
Rate Limiting

Anthropic enforces two types of rate limits: requests per minute (RPM) and tokens per minute (TPM). If you exceed either, you get 429 errors until the next minute window. This matters most when you are processing data in bulk or running multiple agents simultaneously.

Every API response includes headers that tell you where you stand: anthropic-ratelimit-requests-remaining shows how many requests you have left in the current window, and anthropic-ratelimit-tokens-remaining shows your remaining token budget. By reading these headers, you can proactively slow down before hitting 429 errors.

Managing Concurrency

The simplest way to stay within limits is to control concurrency — the number of simultaneous API calls. A request queue with a configurable maximum (e.g., 5-10 concurrent requests) prevents bursts that exceed your rate limit. When a slot frees up, the next queued request starts. This approach is simpler and more reliable than reactive strategies like parsing Retry-After headers after hitting 429 errors. For token-based limits, a token bucket rate limiter lets you budget tokens per minute and delays requests when the bucket is empty.

exercises.question: Implement a request queue with configurable concurrency.
exercises.explanation: Blocks new requests at max concurrency. When one completes, the next starts. Prevents 429 errors.

exercises.question: Which header shows remaining requests in your rate limit window?
exercises.explanation: Anthropic uses anthropic-ratelimit-requests-remaining and anthropic-ratelimit-tokens-remaining.

exercises.question: Build a token bucket rate limiter that tracks tokens per minute.
