exercises.explanation: Token bucket refills over time. When empty, requests wait. Prevents burst-then-429 patterns.

lessons.title: Caching & Cost Optimization
lessons.description: Reduce costs with prompt caching, response caching, and model routing.

sections.content:
Cost Optimization

API costs can grow quickly in production, especially with high-volume workloads. Three strategies can dramatically reduce your bill without sacrificing quality.

Prompt Caching

If you send the same system prompt or context block across many requests, Anthropic's prompt caching gives you 90% savings on those repeated tokens. Mark your cacheable content blocks with cache_control: { type: 'ephemeral' }, and subsequent requests within a 5-minute window reuse the cached content at 10% of the normal input cost. This is especially valuable for applications with long system prompts or RAG systems that include the same reference documents repeatedly.

Response Caching

For deterministic requests (temperature 0) with identical inputs, cache the response locally to avoid calling the API at all. Hash the request parameters to create a cache key, store the response with a TTL, and return the cached result on subsequent identical requests. This works well for classification, extraction, and any task where the same input always produces the same output.

Model Routing

The biggest cost lever is using the right model for each task. Haiku costs roughly 12x less than Sonnet per token. If 70% of your requests are simple enough for Haiku, routing them to the cheaper model saves 60-80% overall. The pattern: use Haiku to classify the request complexity (one cheap call), then route to the appropriate model. This adds a small latency overhead but pays for itself many times over at scale.

exercises.question: Implement a response cache with TTL and hash-based keys.
exercises.explanation: Hash-based caching deduplicates identical requests. TTL prevents stale data. Only for temperature 0.

exercises.question: How much do cached prompt tokens cost compared to uncached?
exercises.explanation: Prompt caching reduces input costs by 90% within a 5-minute window.

exercises.question: Build a model router that classifies complexity and routes to Haiku or Sonnet.
exercises.explanation: Haiku classifies (cheap), then routes. Simple stays on Haiku, complex goes to Sonnet. Saves 60-80% on mixed workloads.

lessons.title: Observability & Monitoring
lessons.description: Log, trace, and monitor AI features in production.

sections.content:
Observability

You cannot improve what you cannot measure. In production AI systems, observability means knowing exactly how every API call performs: how long it took, how many tokens it consumed, how much it cost, whether it succeeded, and why it failed. Without this visibility, you are flying blind — you will not notice degradation until users complain, and you will not know which part of your system is responsible.

Key Metrics

Track these for every API call: latency (p50 for typical performance, p95 for tail latency that affects 5% of users, p99 for worst-case), tokens per request (input and output separately — a sudden spike means something changed), error rate by type (separate 429s from 500s, they have different root causes), cost per request and per user (catch expensive patterns before they blow your budget), and tool call success rate (a drop means a tool or external API is failing).

Structured Logging

Log every API call as structured JSON with model, token counts, cost, latency, stop_reason, and success/failure status. This makes logs queryable — you can answer 'which model costs the most?' or 'what is the p95 latency for Opus requests?' with a simple query. Console.log with formatted strings is insufficient for production. Pipe structured logs to your observability stack (Datadog, Grafana, CloudWatch) and set up alerts on error rate spikes and latency degradation.

exercises.question: Build a wrapper that logs every API call with timing, tokens, cost, and errors.
exercises.explanation: Structured JSON logs enable querying and alerting. Track cost per request to catch expensive patterns.

exercises.question: Most important metric for detecting a degraded AI feature?
exercises.explanation: p95 latency catches tail latency issues that averages hide. A spike indicates degradation for 5% of users.

exercises.question: Create a per-user cost tracker that alerts when budget is exceeded.
exercises.explanation: Per-user tracking prevents runaway spend. Alert or block when budget exceeded.

courses.title: Build a Voice Agent
courses.description: Build a production voice agent using VAPI and Claude: real-time conversation, tool integration, and telephony.

lessons.title: Voice Agent Architecture
lessons.description: The voice pipeline: STT, LLM, TTS, and how VAPI orchestrates them.

sections.content:
Voice Agent Architecture

Voice agents add a real-time audio layer on top of the text-based Claude API. The architecture has four layers that process audio in a continuous loop: STT (Speech-to-Text) converts the caller's audio into text, the LLM (Claude) processes the text and generates a response, TTS (Text-to-Speech) converts Claude's text response back into natural-sounding audio, and Transport delivers the audio to the user via WebRTC (web) or SIP/PSTN (phone).

VAPI is an orchestration platform that handles all four layers, letting you focus on the conversation design rather than audio infrastructure. You configure which STT provider (Deepgram, AssemblyAI), which LLM (Claude), and which TTS voice (ElevenLabs, Cartesia) to use. VAPI manages the real-time audio streaming, turn detection (knowing when the caller has stopped speaking), and interruption handling (when the caller speaks over the agent).

Latency Budget

In voice, every millisecond matters. Humans notice delays over 300ms in conversation. The latency budget is split across all four layers: STT (~100ms), LLM response (~500-1500ms), and TTS (~200ms). This is why short responses are essential for voice agents — a 3-sentence response generates audio faster than a 3-paragraph one. VAPI orchestrates all four layers:

{
  model: { provider: 'anthropic', model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514', systemPrompt: '...' },
  voice: { provider: 'elevenlabs', voiceId: 'rachel' },
  transcriber: { provider: 'deepgram', model: 'nova-3' }
}

exercises.question: Correct order of the voice agent pipeline?
exercises.explanation: Audio in -> STT (text) -> LLM (response) -> TTS (speech) -> Transport (deliver audio).

exercises.question: Define a VAPI assistant config with Claude, ElevenLabs, and Deepgram.
exercises.explanation: Keep maxTokens low (256) for voice. Long responses sound unnatural. firstMessage starts the conversation.

exercises.question: Why keep maxTokens low for voice agents?
options: Long responses sound unnatural in voice
exercises.explanation: Voice conversations are turn-based. Short, conversational responses feel natural.

lessons.title: Webhook Integration
lessons.description: Handle VAPI webhooks for tool calls and end-of-call processing.

sections.content:
Webhooks

While VAPI handles the audio pipeline, your webhook server is where the real business logic lives. VAPI sends HTTP POST requests to your server for key events during a call: when Claude wants to call a tool (function-call), when the call ends (end-of-call-report), and when status changes occur. Your server processes these events and returns results that VAPI feeds back into the conversation.

The most important webhook event is function-call. When Claude decides to use a tool during a voice conversation (check appointment availability, look up a customer record, book a meeting), VAPI sends the tool name and parameters to your webhook. You execute the function and return the result. VAPI passes it back to Claude, which uses it to continue the conversation — all within the flow of a natural phone call. The second critical event is end-of-call-report, which includes the full transcript, call duration, and summary. This is where you save data to your CRM, extract action items, or trigger follow-up workflows. Handle tool calls and post-call processing:

app.post('/webhook', async (req, res) => {
  const { message } = req.body;
  if (message.type === 'function-call') {
    const result = await handleTool(message.functionCall);
    return res.json({ result });
  }
  if (message.type === 'end-of-call-report') {
    await saveReport(message);
  }
  res.json({});
});

exercises.question: Write a webhook handler for 'check_appointment' and 'book_appointment' tools.
exercises.explanation: Route tool calls by name. Return JSON results. VAPI passes results back to Claude.

exercises.question: VAPI webhook event type for tool calls?
exercises.explanation: VAPI uses 'function-call' for tool invocations.

exercises.question: Handle end-of-call: extract action items from transcript using Claude, save to DB.
exercises.explanation: End-of-call processing generates business value. Use Haiku for fast, cheap transcript processing.

lessons.title: Voice-Optimized Prompts
lessons.description: Write prompts for natural conversation: tone, pacing, turn-taking.

sections.content:
Voice Prompts

Writing prompts for voice agents is fundamentally different from writing prompts for text-based applications. In text, Claude can respond with bullet lists, code blocks, and long explanations. In voice, the response is spoken aloud — and anything that reads well on screen sounds terrible when spoken. The rules for voice prompting are non-negotiable if you want a natural-sounding agent.

Key Differences from Text Prompts

  • Under 3 sentences per response — Long responses lose the caller's attention and feel like a lecture. Keep it short and conversational.
  • Conversational language, no jargon/markdown/lists — Bullet points and headers cannot be spoken. Write as if you are having a phone conversation with a friend.
  • End with a question for turn-taking — In phone conversations, the person who asks the question passes the floor. Without a question, there is an awkward silence.
  • One question at a time — Asking 'What is your name, when did this happen, and were you injured?' overwhelms callers. Ask one, wait for the answer, then ask the next.
  • Never say 'as an AI' — Breaking character destroys the conversational illusion. The agent should behave as a natural human assistant.

The Empathy Factor

For sensitive applications (legal intake, medical triage, customer complaints), the prompt must instruct Claude to acknowledge emotions before moving to the next question. 'I am sorry to hear that happened to you. Can you tell me when this occurred?' is far better than 'When did it happen?' Voice agents that skip empathy feel robotic, regardless of how natural the TTS voice sounds.

exercises.question: Write a voice-optimized prompt for a PI law firm intake agent.
exercises.solution:
const intakePrompt = `You are Sarah, an intake specialist at Smith & Associates.

Collect: what happened, when, medical treatment, injuries.

Rules:
- Be warm and empathetic
- One question at a time
- Under 2 sentences plus one question
- Acknowledge feelings before asking next question
- No legal jargon
- Once you have basics, offer free consultation
- Never promise outcomes
- Fees: contingency, no upfront cost

Start by greeting warmly and asking what happened.`;
exercises.explanation: Voice intake needs empathy, one-question flow, and clear boundaries. Short responses keep it natural.

exercises.question: Most important rule for voice agent prompts?
exercises.explanation: Short responses are essential for voice. Long ones break conversational flow.

exercises.question: Create a template system that generates voice prompts from business parameters.
exercises.explanation: Templates standardize voice prompts. Core voice rules stay constant while business details are parameterized.

lessons.title: Web & Phone Integration
lessons.description: Deploy on websites via WebRTC and phone numbers via SIP/PSTN.

sections.content:
Web & Phone Integration

Voice agents can be deployed in two channels: web (embedded in your website) and phone (real phone numbers people can call). Each channel has different technical requirements and user expectations.

Web Integration

The VAPI web SDK handles WebRTC audio streaming in the browser. You initialize it with your public key, start a call with an assistant ID, and listen for events. The SDK manages microphone access, audio encoding, and real-time streaming. Your React component just needs to manage UI state (idle, connecting, active) and provide start/stop buttons:

import Vapi from '@vapi-ai/web';
const vapi = new Vapi('public-key');
vapi.start({ assistantId: 'your-id' });
vapi.on('call-start', () => {});
vapi.on('call-end', () => {});

Phone Integration

VAPI provisions phone numbers that connect to your assistants. Inbound calls arrive at your webhook, where you handle tool calls and post-call processing. You can also make outbound calls via the REST API — useful for appointment reminders, follow-ups, or outbound sales.

Web vs. Phone Prompts

A common best practice is running separate assistants for web and phone. Web users expect instant responses and shorter conversations (lean prompt, fewer questions). Phone callers expect a more thorough, empathetic conversation (detailed prompt, full intake flow). Same agent logic, different prompt tuning for each channel.

exercises.question: Build a React component with VAPI web SDK: call button and status display.
exercises.explanation: VAPI SDK handles WebRTC and audio. Your component just manages UI state and lifecycle.

exercises.question: VAPI web SDK transport?
exercises.explanation: WebRTC provides low-latency peer-to-peer audio streaming in the browser.

exercises.question: Write a function to make an outbound call via VAPI API.
exercises.explanation: Outbound calls use the REST API. The assistant handles the conversation once connected.

lessons.title: Voice Agent Case Study
lessons.description: End-to-end build: assistant config, webhook server, tools, monitoring, deployment.

sections.content:
Case Study: Law Firm Intake Agent

To tie everything together, let us walk through a real-world voice agent: an intake specialist for a personal injury law firm. This agent answers calls, empathetically gathers case details, checks attorney availability, and books consultations — all without human intervention.

Architecture Choices

  • STT: Deepgram nova-3 — Chosen for accuracy with legal terminology (plaintiff, defendant, personal injury, statute of limitations).
  • LLM: Claude Sonnet — Balanced speed and quality. Opus would be more accurate but too slow for voice latency requirements.
  • TTS: ElevenLabs — Natural, warm voice that conveys empathy. Voice selection matters for sensitive intake conversations.
  • Tools: check_availability (calendar lookup), book_consultation (CRM integration), lookup_case_type (classify the injury type from the caller's description).
  • Dual assistants: Separate web (lean prompt for fast responses on the website widget) and phone (full prompt with complete intake flow for callers) configurations.

End-to-End Flow

Caller dials in -> VAPI connects -> agent greets warmly and asks what happened -> caller describes the accident -> agent asks one follow-up at a time (when, injuries, medical treatment) -> agent classifies the case type via tool -> checks availability -> books a consultation -> confirms details. After the call, the end-of-call webhook saves the transcript, extracted action items, and booking details to the CRM. The attorney walks into the consultation fully briefed.

exercises.question: Build the complete webhook server with all 3 tools and end-of-call handling.
exercises.explanation: The webhook is the brain behind the voice agent. Handles tools in real-time and processes data after calls end.

exercises.question: Why separate web and phone assistants?
options: Different prompts: lean for web speed, detailed for phone depth
exercises.explanation: Web needs instant responses (lean prompt). Phone callers expect thorough conversations (detailed prompt).

exercises.question: Build a metrics tracker for voice agents: call volume, duration, conversion rate, tool usage.
exercises.explanation: Track conversion rate, average duration, and tool usage. Essential for optimizing voice agent performance.
